content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "Plants vs. Zombies\u2122 2",
  "game_name": "Grid Garden Defenders",
  "genre": "Tower Defense",
  "core_mechanics": "The game operates on a 5-lane, 9-column grid. Players manage 'sun' currency to deploy plants, which automatically attack or defend against incoming waves of zombies. Sun is generated automatically and through planted Sunflowers. Zombies move leftward along lanes, and if they reach the leftmost column (the 'house'), the player loses. Plants have health and can be 'eaten' by zombies. Projectiles fired by offensive plants damage zombies. Each plant type has a sun cost and a cooldown period after placement. Plant Food is a limited-use power-up that temporarily supercharges a plant's ability. Gameplay involves selecting plants from a seed packet bar, placing them on the grid, and managing sun resources to withstand increasingly difficult zombie waves. State tracking includes: current sun amount, plant availability and cooldowns, plant positions and health on the grid, zombie positions, types, health, and current targets, active projectiles, sun drops on screen, current wave number, and the overall game state (menu, playing, paused, etc.).",
  "visual_elements": "The game will be rendered using p5.js. The main game area is a 900x500 pixel canvas (9 columns x 5 lanes, 100x100 pixels per cell). \n\n**Grid:** A light green background with darker green grid lines defining 5 rows (lanes) and 9 columns.\n\n**UI Elements:**\n*   **Seed Packet Bar:** Located at the top of the screen. Rectangular slots (e.g., 80x100px) displaying plant icons, sun cost, and cooldown status. \n*   **Sun Display:** Top-left corner, displaying 'Sun: [current_sun_amount]' as yellow text.\n*   **Plant Food Indicator:** Top-right corner, displaying a small icon and count 'PF: [count]'.\n*   **Score Display:** Top-right corner, below Plant Food, displaying 'Score: [current_score]' as white text.\n*   **Level Indicator:** Top-left corner, below Sun Display, displaying 'Level: [current_level]' as white text.\n*   **Game Messages:** Central screen overlay for 'Wave X', 'Level Complete!', 'Game Over', 'You Win!', 'Paused'.\n\n**Game Objects (Representations):**\n*   **Sun Drops:** Small yellow circles (e.g., diameter 30px).\n*   **Plants:**\n    *   **Sunflower:** Yellow circle (diameter 60px) with small brown center, green stem.\n    *   **Peashooter:** Green circle (diameter 60px) with a small dark green 'mouth' for shooting, green stem.\n    *   **Wall-nut:** Brown square (60x60px) with a cracked texture (simple darker brown lines) to indicate damage.\n*   **Zombies:**\n    *   **Basic Zombie:** Grey rectangle (60x80px) with two small white circles for eyes and a red line for mouth. Simple walking animation (slight bobbing/jitter).\n    *   **Conehead Zombie:** Basic Zombie shape with an orange triangle (cone) on its head.\n*   **Projectiles (Peas):** Small green circles (diameter 10px), moving rightward.\n*   **Plant Food Effect:** When activated, a selected plant emits a brief yellow aura/glow animation.\n*   **Damage/Death Effects:** Zombies disappear with a simple 'poof' particle effect (small grey/green circles scattering). Plants eaten by zombies visually shrink or become broken.\n\n**Animations/Effects:**\n*   **Planting:** A plant appears with a slight scale-up effect upon placement.\n*   **Sun Collection:** Sun drops visually move towards the sun counter when collected.\n*   **Peashooter Firing:** Small projectile travels from plant to zombie. \n*   **Zombie Eating:** Zombie model jitters or makes small lunging movements towards the plant it's attacking.\n*   **Plant Food Activation:** Selected plant glows briefly.\n*   **Game Over/Win:** Full-screen overlays with appropriate text and options.",
  "control_scheme": "Given the 'streamline resource management using keyboard shortcuts' requirement and HTML5 context:\n\n*   **Number Keys (1, 2, 3):** Select plants from the seed packet bar. `1` for the leftmost plant, `2` for the second, etc. (up to 3 plants in early levels, expanding later). When a plant is selected, a ghost image of the plant follows the mouse cursor, snapped to the grid. \n*   **Mouse Click (Left):** Place the currently selected plant onto the grid at the cursor's location. If the cursor is not on the grid, or if conditions (sun cost, cooldown) are not met, the click does nothing. Also used to click 'Start Game', 'Next Level', etc. on UI screens.\n*   **Shift:** Activate Plant Food mode. When pressed, the player can hover over an eligible plant with the mouse, and a highlight will appear. Clicking the mouse will then apply Plant Food to that plant.\n*   **Escape:** Toggle Pause/Unpause game. During the paused state, a 'PAUSED' overlay is displayed, and game logic is frozen.\n*   **Sun Collection:** Automatic; sun drops are collected when they fall onto the lawn and are 'tapped' visually by the game system, adding to the sun currency.",
  "game_states": "The game will transition through the following states:\n\n1.  **LOADING:** (Initial state) Displays a simple 'Loading...' message while assets and p5.js setup are prepared.\n    *   **Trigger:** Application start.\n    *   **Transition:** Automatically moves to `MAIN_MENU` once setup is complete.\n2.  **MAIN_MENU:** Displays 'Grid Garden Defenders' title, 'Start Game' button, 'High Scores' button (simplified to just display the top score).\n    *   **Trigger:** From `LOADING`, `GAME_OVER`, or `WIN_SCREEN`.\n    *   **Transition:** Clicking 'Start Game' leads to `LEVEL_SELECT`.\n3.  **LEVEL_SELECT:** Displays buttons for available levels (Level 1, Level 2, etc., up to 5). \n    *   **Trigger:** From `MAIN_MENU`.\n    *   **Transition:** Clicking a level button initiates loading for that level and transitions to `PLAYING`.\n4.  **PLAYING:** The main gameplay loop. Plants and zombies interact, sun falls, waves progress.\n    *   **Trigger:** From `LEVEL_SELECT`.\n    *   **Transition:**\n        *   Zombie reaches house -> `GAME_OVER`.\n        *   All waves defeated -> `LEVEL_CLEARED`.\n        *   `Escape` key pressed -> `PAUSED`.\n5.  **PAUSED:** Game logic is suspended, 'PAUSED' overlay is shown, possibly with 'Resume', 'Restart Level', 'Main Menu' options.\n    *   **Trigger:** `Escape` key pressed during `PLAYING`.\n    *   **Transition:**\n        *   `Escape` key pressed / 'Resume' clicked -> `PLAYING`.\n        *   'Restart Level' clicked -> `PLAYING` (restarts current level).\n        *   'Main Menu' clicked -> `MAIN_MENU`.\n6.  **LEVEL_CLEARED:** All zombie waves for the current level are defeated. Displays 'Level X Complete!' and the score for that level, plus options.\n    *   **Trigger:** Last zombie of the final wave of a level is defeated during `PLAYING`.\n    *   **Transition:**\n        *   If not the final level: 'Next Level' button (or auto-advance after 3s) -> `PLAYING` (loads next level).\n        *   If final level: 'View Scores' / 'Main Menu' button -> `WIN_SCREEN`.\n7.  **GAME_OVER:** A zombie has reached the house. Displays 'GAME OVER!', final score, and options.\n    *   **Trigger:** Any zombie reaches the leftmost column during `PLAYING`.\n    *   **Transition:** 'Restart Level' clicked -> `PLAYING` (restarts current level). 'Main Menu' clicked -> `MAIN_MENU`.\n8.  **WIN_SCREEN:** All levels completed. Displays 'YOU WIN!', total score, and high score.\n    *   **Trigger:** Completing the final level (`LEVEL_CLEARED` state for the last level).\n    *   **Transition:** 'Main Menu' clicked -> `MAIN_MENU`.",
  "win_conditions": "The player wins the current level by successfully defeating all waves of zombies for that level, preventing any zombie from reaching the house. The entire game is won by successfully completing all 5 defined levels.",
  "lose_conditions": "The player loses the current level (resulting in a 'GAME OVER') if any zombie reaches the leftmost column (the 'house') of the grid.",
  "scoring_system": "The game incorporates a cumulative scoring system, with points displayed in the top-right corner as 'Score: [current_score]'.\n\n*   **Points Awarded:**\n    *   `+10` points for each Basic Zombie defeated.\n    *   `+20` points for each Conehead Zombie defeated.\n    *   `+5` points for each sun drop collected (automatically).\n    *   `+50` points for completing each wave of zombies.\n    *   `+100` points for completing a level.\n*   **Bonus Points:**\n    *   `+50` points for each plant remaining on the grid at the end of a level (i.e., not eaten by zombies).\n    *   `+100` points 'Perfect Defense' bonus if no zombies reached the house during the entire level.\n*   **Multipliers:** No score multipliers are included to maintain simplification.\n*   **High Score System:** The highest cumulative score achieved across all play sessions will be tracked using `localStorage` and displayed on the `MAIN_MENU` and `GAME_OVER`/`WIN_SCREEN` screens.\n*   **Display:** The current score will be prominently displayed in the top-right corner of the screen during the `PLAYING` state: `Score: [current_score]`.\n*   **Gameplay Effect:** The score does not directly affect gameplay; it is solely a measure of player achievement and a competition metric for high scores.",
  "level_system": "The game features 5 distinct levels, each progressively increasing in difficulty and introducing new challenges. The current level is displayed in the top-left corner as 'Level: [current_level]'.\n\n**General Mechanics:** All levels use a 5-lane, 9-column grid. Players begin each level with a base amount of sun and can use Sunflowers (cost 50 sun) to generate more sun (25 sun/drop). Peashooters cost 100 sun and deal 20 damage per projectile. Wall-nuts cost 50 sun and have 400 health. Basic Zombies have 100 health. Conehead Zombies have 250 health.\n\n*   **Level 1: \"Sunny Start\"**\n    *   **Objective:** Introduce core mechanics and basic plant types.\n    *   **Available Plants:** Sunflower, Peashooter.\n    *   **Zombies:** Basic Zombie only (speed: 1 unit/sec).\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** Very Easy. 3 short waves. Each wave has 2-3 zombies.\n    *   **Starting Sun:** 75.\n    *   **Sun Drop Frequency:** Every 8-12 seconds (auto-collected).\n    *   **Advancement:** Complete all 3 waves.\n    *   **Transition Display:** \"Level 1 Complete!\" followed by a 'Next Level' button.\n\n*   **Level 2: \"Nutty Defense\"**\n    *   **Objective:** Introduce defensive plants (Wall-nut) and a new, tougher zombie type.\n    *   **Available Plants:** Sunflower, Peashooter, Wall-nut.\n    *   **Zombies:** Basic Zombie (speed: 1 unit/sec), Conehead Zombie (speed: 0.8 unit/sec, appears in later waves).\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** Easy. 4 waves. Waves 3 and 4 introduce 1-2 Conehead Zombies.\n    *   **Starting Sun:** 50.\n    *   **Sun Drop Frequency:** Every 10-15 seconds.\n    *   **Advancement:** Complete all 4 waves.\n    *   **Transition Display:** \"Level 2 Complete!\" followed by a 'Next Level' button.\n\n*   **Level 3: \"Pea Power\"**\n    *   **Objective:** Challenge players with increased zombie numbers and slightly faster basic zombies.\n    *   **Available Plants:** Sunflower, Peashooter, Wall-nut.\n    *   **Zombies:** Basic Zombie (speed: 1.2 unit/sec), Conehead Zombie (speed: 0.8 unit/sec, more frequent).\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** Medium. 5 waves. Waves are longer, with 4-6 zombies each, including 2-3 Conehead Zombies in later waves.\n    *   **Starting Sun:** 25.\n    *   **Sun Drop Frequency:** Every 12-18 seconds.\n    *   **Advancement:** Complete all 5 waves.\n    *   **Transition Display:** \"Level 3 Complete!\" followed by a 'Next Level' button.\n\n*   **Level 4: \"Strategic Stand\"**\n    *   **Objective:** Requires more careful planning and resource management due to increased pressure.\n    *   **Available Plants:** Sunflower, Peashooter, Wall-nut.\n    *   **Zombies:** Basic Zombie (speed: 1.5 unit/sec), Conehead Zombie (speed: 1 unit/sec, more numerous).\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** Hard. 6 waves. Waves are significantly longer and contain mixed groups of 6-8 zombies, with multiple Conehead Zombies per lane.\n    *   **Starting Sun:** 0.\n    *   **Sun Drop Frequency:** Every 15-20 seconds.\n    *   **Advancement:** Complete all 6 waves.\n    *   **Transition Display:** \"Level 4 Complete!\" followed by a 'Next Level' button.\n\n*   **Level 5: \"Final Frontier\"**\n    *   **Objective:** The ultimate test, demanding optimal plant placement and quick reactions.\n    *   **Available Plants:** Sunflower, Peashooter, Wall-nut.\n    *   **Zombies:** Basic Zombie (speed: 1.8 unit/sec), Conehead Zombie (speed: 1.2 unit/sec, very numerous, often in groups).\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** Very Hard. 7 waves. Relentless waves of 8-10 zombies, with heavy concentrations of Conehead Zombies and fast Basic Zombies.\n    *   **Starting Sun:** 0.\n    *   **Sun Drop Frequency:** Every 18-25 seconds.\n    *   **Advancement:** Complete all 7 waves. This transitions to the `WIN_SCREEN`.",
  "simplifications": "To create a manageable HTML5 implementation, the following aspects of the original Plants vs. Zombies 2 concept have been simplified:\n\n*   **Resource Management:** Sun collection is automatic. Plant selection and placement heavily utilize keyboard shortcuts (1, 2, 3) for quick deployment, streamlining the original's tap-then-drag.\n*   **Plant/Zombie Variety:** Reduced significantly. Only 3 plant types (Sunflower, Peashooter, Wall-nut) and 2 zombie types (Basic Zombie, Conehead Zombie) are included. Complex plants with special area-of-effect or explosive abilities (e.g., Cherry Bomb, Spikeweed) and advanced zombies (e.g., Gargantuar, Dolphin Rider) are omitted.\n*   **Game Progression:** Instead of 'over 300 levels across 11 distinct worlds', the game is streamlined to 5 progressively difficult levels within a single environment. There is no world map or branching progression.\n*   **Plant Upgrades/Seed Packets:** The entire 'Seed Packets earned to upgrade plant stats' system is removed. Plant stats (damage, health, cost) are fixed throughout the game.\n*   **Plant Food Mechanics:** Simplified. Plant Food is a universal power-up (not specific to certain plants) and does not have varying effects per plant; it provides a generic, temporary supercharge (e.g., increased firing rate or defense for a short duration). Its collection is simplified to occasional drops rather than specific zombie kills or mini-games.\n*   **Secondary Goals:** The primary objective is always to prevent zombies from reaching the house. Complex secondary goals like 'protect specific plants' or 'don't lose more than X plants' are removed.\n*   **Visuals and Audio:** Focus on functional, simple geometric shapes and colors with basic animations rather than detailed sprites and extensive visual effects. Audio will be minimal or omitted initially.\n*   **UI/Meta-Game Features:** Features like the Almanac, Zen Garden, in-game store, or daily challenges are completely removed. The game focuses purely on the core tower defense gameplay loop.\n*   **Multiplayer:** The original PvZ2 is single-player; this implementation remains single-player.",
  "implementation_notes": "The game will be built using p5.js, leveraging its drawing and event handling capabilities. Matter.js is not strictly necessary for this simplified, grid-based, lane-oriented game, as custom collision detection and movement logic will suffice. \n\n**Score Display:** The current score will be an integer value, displayed in the format 'Score: XXXX' at the top-right corner of the canvas during the `PLAYING` state. A high score will be stored using `localStorage`.\n\n**Level Progression:** Upon successfully completing a level (defeating all waves), a 'Level X Complete!' message will briefly appear centrally. If it's not the final level, a 'Next Level' button will appear, or the game will auto-advance to the next level after a short delay (e.g., 3 seconds). If it's the final level, the game will transition to the `WIN_SCREEN`.\n\n**Game Loop:** The `setup()` function will initialize the game state, load initial assets, and set up the canvas. The `draw()` function will contain the main game loop, handling state updates, rendering, and input processing based on the current `game_state`. Object-oriented programming will be used, with classes for `Plant`, `Zombie`, `Projectile`, `SunDrop`, and `GameManager` to manage overall game flow and objects. Collision detection will be handled via bounding box checks for grid-based interactions (zombies vs. plants) and simple distance checks for projectiles."
}
</task>
<example_html>
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>
      <div class="control-buttons">
        <button id="humanModeBtn" class="control-button active" onclick="window.setControlMode('HUMAN')">Human Mode</button>
        <button id="test_1_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_1')">TEST (Basic testing)</button>
        <button id="test_2_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_2')">TEST (Win)</button>
      </div>
      <p id="gameDescription" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_description}</p>
      <p id="gameControls" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_controls}</p>
    </div>
    <script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/p5.js/1.4.0/p5.js"></script>
    <script src="https://unpkg.com/p5.collide2d@0.7.3/p5.collide2d.js"></script>
    <script type="module" src="game.js"></script>
  </body>
</html>

</example_html>

<output_instructions>
Output the code plan and game files in this format with NO OTHER TEXT:

<game_description>
... (Describe the game to the player, the objective, what they need to know to play the game. Do not mention the controls here. Keep it short and informative.)

Grid Garden Defenders is a lane-based tower defense game where you must protect your garden from waves of zombies. Plant Sunflowers to generate sun currency, deploy Peashooters to attack zombies, and use Wall-nuts as defensive barriers. Zombies march down 5 lanes trying to reach your house. If any zombie reaches the left edge, you lose! Complete all 5 increasingly challenging levels to win. Manage your sun resources wisely and place plants strategically to defend against Basic and Conehead Zombies. Grid Garden Defenders

Grid Garden Defenders is a lane-based tower defense game where you must protect your garden from waves of zombies. Plant Sunflowers to generate sun currency, deploy Peashooters to attack zombies, and use Wall-nuts as defensive barriers. Zombies march down 5 lanes trying to reach your house. If any zombie reaches the left edge, you lose! Complete all 5 increasingly challenging levels to win. Manage your sun resources wisely and place plants strategically to defend against Basic and Conehead Zombies.

ENTER: Start the game from the main menu | 1, 2, 3: Select plant type (Sunflower, Peashooter, Wall-nut) | Arrow Keys: Move plant placement cursor on the grid | SPACE: Place selected plant at cursor position | Z: Activate Plant Food on the plant at cursor position (if available) | ESC: Pause/Unpause the game | R: Restart and return to main menu
